quote: Moon Duo return with their second proper full length, and if you needed any further proof of their masterful grasp of fuzzed out hypnotic grooves, well, look no further. It's almost as if Moon Duo set out to link every one of their records together as one gigantic pulsating trip into your mind, and Mazes takes you further in.
A staggering, career-defining record. Dark, dense, political, and utterly mesmerizing. Produced by Oneohtrix Point Never and Daniele Luppi, it's a masterpiece of modern electronic music.
Frantically spaced, fuzzed out and absolutely fucking ridiculous in the best possible way. One of the foundational records of the undergroundkosmische movement.
Dense, propulsive, occasionally funky. One of the great statements in motorik rhythm and free-form exploration. A landmark recording from start to finish.
Relentless, driving afrobeat with a political edge that still cuts deep decades later. The title track is twenty-six minutes of relentless groove that never lets up.
From the man who invented a new way to write. Dense, fragmentary, compulsively readable. Like stepping into a fever dream written on the edge of consciousness.
Still the most devastating piece of music ever recorded. Nothing else comes close. A spiritual journey in four parts, building to an almost unbearable intensity.
Creeping, repetitive, monolithic. The kind of record that makes you forget what year it is. Pure audio hypnosis from start to finish.
The godfather of Afrofuturism at his most cosmic and accessible. Jazzy, spacey, deeply strange and utterly magnificent. An essential document.
Glittering, sleazy, grandiose. Bowie at his most theatrical and unhinged. Part concept album, part apocalypse, all genius.
Radically re-contextualizing music from the 12th to the 18th century, played with an intensity and modern sensibility that makes it sound completely fresh.

album: Onze Dansez Pour Combattre La Migraine
quote: Weird, wonderful, totally unique. Crammed Discs at their best. Part funk, part avant-garde, part minimalist, all fascinating.

album: Complete Columbia: Live At University Of Missouri 4/25/93
quote: Over two hours of live Big Star, the band that invented everything. Alex Chilton at his most raw and powerful, backed by Jon Auer and Ken Stringer.
Mysterious private press soul-jazz-funk, deep and dark and funky. One of those records you hear once and immediately need to own.
Lo-fi minimal synth music from the early 80s, before minimal synth was even a thing. Sparse, paranoid, and strangely beautiful.
Heavy, driving, melodic post-punk. Big fuzzy bass, pounding drums, and soaring vocals. The kind of record that makes you want to punch a wall and dance.
Driving, repetitive krautrock with moments of pure cosmic freakout. One of the essential German records from the golden era of kosmische.
Over two hours of devastating, crushing drone-doom. Boris at their heaviest and most experimental. Not for the faint of heart.

album: Quarter Turns Over The Living Area
quote: Deep, atmospheric, percussive post-punk. One of the most anticipated records of the year, and it delivers on every level.
Hazy, beautiful, cosmic. Two masters of drone and kosmische coming together for a record that feels like swimming in warm sunlight.
Dense, droning, noise-rock beauty. One of the most original and influential bands of the decade, at their most focused and powerful.
Creepy, theatrical, hook-laden art-rock. Destroyer at his most immediate and accessible. Dan Bejar at his absolute best.
Twisted, theatrical, glam-soaked indie rock. Big hooks, bigger arrangements, and some of the most ambitious songwriting you'll hear all year.
Funk-soaked Ethiopian grooves, heavy on the hypnotic rhythms and fuzzy guitars. Impossible not to move to.
Dark, atmospheric, devastating black metal. Deeply complex and surprisingly melodic. One of the most compelling records in the genre.
